condition_overview.summary: Generalised Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is a chronic psychiatric condition characterised by persistent, excessive, and difficult-to-control worry about a wide range of topics, accompanied by physiological and cognitive symptoms including muscle tension, restlessness, fatigue, irritability, concentration difficulties, and sleep disturbance. Neuroimaging and clinical neuroscience research commonly implicate altered threat processing and prefrontal–limbic interactions, but findings are not used diagnostically. GAD is common in clinical and community settings and often co-occurs with depression and sleep problems.
condition_overview.prevalence: U.S. national survey data summarized by NIMH (NCS-R, DSM-IV diagnoses) report past-year prevalence of generalized anxiety disorder of about 2.7% in adults (females 3.4%, males 1.9%) and lifetime prevalence of about 5.7%. These figures describe the surveyed era and diagnostic instrument; DSM-5 prevalence estimates may differ slightly. GAD is associated with substantial role impairment in community samples.

phenotype_clusters.name: High Arousal / Hypervigilance
phenotype_clusters.description: Prominent physiological overactivation: restlessness, startle responses, muscle tension, racing heart, and sleep-onset difficulty. ANS dysregulation drives the clinical picture. Responds well to CES and HRV biofeedback.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated frontal and central beta (>15 Hz); reduced alpha at Pz/Oz; low HRV

phenotype_clusters.name: Somatic Anxiety
phenotype_clusters.description: Anxiety manifests predominantly through physical symptoms: muscle tension, GI disturbance, headaches, fatigue. Worry content may centre on health. High overlap with somatic symptom disorder.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated theta-alpha transition instability; reduced posterior alpha; ANS dysregulation

phenotype_clusters.name: Cognitive Worry-Dominant
phenotype_clusters.description: Excessive uncontrollable worry as the primary presenting feature, with relative absence of overt physiological symptoms. Rumination is prominent. Often misattributed to depression. Responds to neurofeedback and cognitive-focused interventions.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Elevated frontal midline theta; reduced right posterior alpha; reduced alpha/theta ratio

phenotype_clusters.name: Mixed Anxiety-Depression
phenotype_clusters.description: GAD co-occurring with significant depressive symptoms, often representing the most disabling phenotype. Functional impairment is disproportionate to either anxiety or depression alone. TMS and CES both applicable.
phenotype_clusters.qeeg_signature: Frontal alpha asymmetry (left > right); elevated frontal theta; reduced frontal beta

symptom_clusters: Excessive worry (difficult to control, about multiple topics)
Restlessness or feeling keyed up/on edge
Sleep disturbance (difficulty falling or staying asleep, restless sleep)
Difficulty concentrating or mind going blank
condition_overview.neurobiology: Neuroimaging and cognitive neuroscience models (widely cited in anxiety disorder reviews) emphasise altered amygdala–prefrontal regulation and attention to threat; heart rate variability often is reduced in clinical anxiety samples and is a physiologic target for HRV biofeedback training. Quantitative EEG patterns (for example relative beta or alpha metrics) are heterogeneous across individuals and are not diagnostic of GAD. Mechanisms proposed for cranial electrotherapy stimulation (CES) devices are not fully established; clinical effects, where present, require independent replication in rigorously blinded trials.
comorbidities: Major depressive disorder (up to 60% lifetime comorbidity)
Irritable bowel syndrome and other functional somatic conditions
Substance use disorders (self-medication with alcohol, benzodiazepines)
differential_diagnoses: Panic disorder (episodic vs. chronic diffuse anxiety)
Social anxiety disorder (situationally-bound vs. generalised worry)
Separation anxiety disorder (focus of worry on attachment figures)
OCD (intrusive ego-dystonic thoughts vs. GAD's ego-syntonic worry)
Depression with anxious distress specifier (rule out primary MDD)
Hyperthyroidism and other medical causes of anxiety symptoms

patient_friendly_explanation.what_is_it: Generalised Anxiety Disorder (GAD) is a condition where your brain gets stuck in a pattern of excessive, difficult-to-control worrying about everyday things — work, health, family, finances — even when there is no immediate threat. It is a recognised medical condition involving how your brain and nervous system regulate threat responses, not a sign of weakness or over-sensitivity.
patient_friendly_explanation.how_it_affects_you: GAD can affect you both mentally and physically. You may find yourself worrying for hours even when you want to stop, feeling constantly on edge or tense, struggling to sleep, having muscle aches and tension headaches, feeling tired most of the time, and finding it hard to concentrate. These symptoms can interfere with work, relationships, and your overall quality of life.
patient_friendly_explanation.treatment_options_overview: Clinical guidelines typically recommend evidence-based psychotherapy (especially CBT adapted for anxiety) and/or medications such as SSRIs or SNRIs for GAD; your clinician will discuss what fits your situation. Some people explore adjunctive neuromodulation or physiologic training. Cranial electrotherapy stimulation (CES) devices are marketed in some countries with regulatory clearance for anxiety/insomnia indications; effects in research trials are mixed and should be discussed honestly, including uncertainty. Heart-rate variability (HRV) biofeedback uses paced breathing with feedback to modify autonomic tone. Neurofeedback is an adjunctive option with variable evidence depending on protocol and population.
patient_friendly_explanation.what_to_expect: If you are using Alpha-Stim CES at home, you will attach small clips to your earlobes and run a 60-minute session once daily. The sensation is very mild — most people feel nothing, or a slight tingling. The device is small and portable. A full course runs for 3–5 weeks. HRV biofeedback involves attending clinic sessions or using a home device to practise slow breathing (about 6 breaths per minute) with real-time feedback from a sensor on your finger or ear. Your clinician will monitor your progress throughout the course.

faq.question: Is Alpha-Stim CES the same as electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)?
faq.answer: No — they are completely different. ECT uses high-current electricity to induce a controlled seizure under general anaesthesia. CES uses a current so small (100 microamps — about 1/1000th of a milliamp) that most people cannot feel it. CES is non-invasive, requires no anaesthesia, and you can use it at home.

faq.question: Can I use CES alongside my anxiety medication?
faq.answer: Yes. CES can be used alongside SSRIs, SNRIs, or buspirone. It is not recommended to use CES while taking benzodiazepines if you are planning to reduce your benzodiazepine dose, as the interaction during withdrawal may cause confusion. Always discuss your full medication list with your clinician.

faq.question: How quickly does CES work?
faq.answer: Many people notice a reduction in anxiety within 1–3 sessions. For GAD, sustained improvement typically builds over 3–5 weeks of daily use. The effects tend to be cumulative — regular use produces better results than occasional use.

faq.question: What is HRV biofeedback and how does it help anxiety?
faq.answer: HRV (Heart Rate Variability) biofeedback trains you to breathe at your body's resonance frequency — typically around 6 breaths per minute. This breathing pattern naturally activates your parasympathetic nervous system (the calming system), reducing the fight-or-flight response that drives anxiety. A sensor monitors your heart rhythm in real time and shows you whether your breathing is hitting the target. With practice, your nervous system becomes better at self-regulating.

faq.question: Do I need to attend a clinic for these treatments?
faq.answer: Alpha-Stim CES can be prescribed for home use after an initial clinic setup and training session. HRV biofeedback can start at the clinic and then transition to home practice with a portable device. Neurofeedback sessions are typically clinic-based.

required_fields.field: Confirmed GAD diagnosis (DSM-5 criteria: ≥6 months excessive worry, ≥3 somatic/cognitive symptoms, significant distress or impairment)
required_fields.reason: Protocol generation requires a confirmed GAD diagnosis.
Primary outcome measure; must be established before treatment commences.

required_fields.field: Current medications (SSRIs, SNRIs, benzodiazepines, buspirone, anticonvulsants, any OTC supplements)
required_fields.reason: Benzodiazepines may interact with CES; anticonvulsants lower seizure threshold; medication review required for TMS safety.
CES contraindicated with cardiac pacemakers. Arrhythmia may be exacerbated by HRV biofeedback protocols — cardiologist review required.

required_fields.field: Skin condition at earlobe electrode sites (for CES)
required_fields.reason: Active skin lesions or infection at earlobes contraindicate CES electrode placement.
Seizure history is an absolute contraindication for TMS; relative for CES. Must be documented before any neuromodulation protocol.

required_fields.field: Prior anxiety treatment history (CBT attempts, medication trials, biofeedback)
required_fields.reason: Documents treatment resistance level and informs protocol selection.

required_fields.field: Sleep disorder history or ISI score
required_fields.reason: Insomnia is highly comorbid with GAD and may need concurrent treatment; may affect CES session timing.

clinical_interview_checklist: Duration of worry episodes (must be ≥6 months for GAD diagnosis)
Worry content — range of topics (vs. focused worry suggesting specific phobia or health anxiety)
Ability to control worry — distinguish GAD from normal worry
Physical symptom burden: muscle tension, fatigue, GI symptoms, headaches
Sleep assessment: sleep-onset difficulty, night waking, early morning waking
Functional impairment: work performance, social activity, self-care
Caffeine and stimulant intake (significant dose-dependent anxiogenic effect)
Alcohol use — many GAD patients self-medicate with alcohol
CBT engagement history and response to thought-challenging techniques
Recent life stressors or ongoing stressors perpetuating anxiety
Suicidal ideation screening (GAD-related suffering elevates risk)
rule_out_differentials: Hyperthyroidism (restlessness, palpitations, fatigue — thyroid function tests required)
Major depressive disorder with anxious distress specifier
Panic disorder (episodic vs. chronic GAD anxiety)
comorbidity_screen: Depression (PHQ-9 — comorbid MDD in up to 60%; affects treatment sequence)
Insomnia (ISI — comorbid insomnia highly prevalent; CES may assist sleep as secondary benefit)
Panic disorder (panic attack frequency and agoraphobia screening)
Alcohol and substance use (AUDIT-C — alcohol self-medication is common in GAD)
Somatic symptom disorder (PHQ-15 if significant physical symptom burden)
prior_treatment_required: Documentation of prior first-line treatments where clinically appropriate (commonly CBT or other structured psychotherapy, and/or SSRI/SNRI pharmacotherapy per guideline-based care)
CBT engagement or documented barriers to CBT access
Benzodiazepine use history and current status (reduction plan if applicable)

additional_requirements: Obstetrician clearance before CES or TMS
CES: limited safety data in pregnancy — discuss risk-benefit; many clinicians defer to post-partum
HRV biofeedback is generally considered safe in pregnancy — confirm with obstetric team
Cardiac history review before CES (common comorbidities: AF, pacemaker)
Baseline cognitive assessment — distinguish GAD from anxiety secondary to cognitive decline
Start CES at lowest current (50 µA) given potentially increased sensitivity

absolute.rationale: CES electrical current may interfere with pacemaker function. Absolute contraindication for CES. Also absolute for TMS.

absolute.condition: Active epilepsy or uncontrolled seizure disorder
absolute.rationale: TMS protocols are contraindicated. CES is a relative contraindication requiring neurologist clearance.
Neuromodulation may exacerbate positive symptoms or mania. Psychiatric stabilisation required before any protocol.

relative.rationale: Insufficient safety data for CES and TMS in pregnancy. HRV biofeedback is likely safe.
relative.mitigation: Obstetrician clearance required. CES may be considered if benefits outweigh risks in severe GAD. Prefer HRV biofeedback or CBT during pregnancy.

relative.condition: Current benzodiazepine use (high dose or daily)
relative.rationale: Benzodiazepines may attenuate CES effects. Concurrent withdrawal increases CNS instability.
relative.mitigation: Stable benzodiazepine dose for ≥4 weeks before commencing neuromodulation. If tapering benzos, complete taper before starting CES or schedule carefully with prescribing clinician.

relative.rationale: HRV biofeedback protocols targeting resonance frequency breathing may be contraindicated with significant arrhythmia as biofeedback feedback may not reflect true HRV.
relative.mitigation: Cardiologist review before HRV biofeedback. CES preferred over HRV biofeedback for this group.

relative.condition: Active skin lesion or infection at earlobe sites
relative.rationale: CES electrodes clip to earlobes; active skin infection or wounds at contact sites increase risk of irritation or infection spread.
relative.mitigation: Delay CES until skin lesions healed. Alternative: behind-ear placement if earlobes affected (check device manual).

relative.rationale: Contraindicated for TMS protocols. Not relevant for CES or HRV biofeedback.
relative.mitigation: Exclude TMS from protocol options. CES and HRV biofeedback remain available.

special_populations.flag: Alpha-Stim CES FDA clearance is for adults. Adolescent use is off-label. Parental consent and paediatric clinician supervision required.
Pacemaker check mandatory before CES. Review all cardiac medications for interactions.

ces: Do not use if cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator is present
Do not apply electrodes over open wounds or infected skin at earlobe sites
Avoid in patients with active ear infection
Do not use while driving or operating heavy machinery
Reduce current to minimum (50 µA) in older adults and those with high sensitivity
hrv-biofeedback: Contraindicated in severe uncontrolled cardiac arrhythmia — standard HRV metrics will be unreliable
Patients with COPD or respiratory conditions should complete biofeedback only under clinical supervision
Do not attempt resonance breathing if resting HR >100 without medical clearance
neurofeedback: Active psychosis is a relative contraindication
Ensure adequate scalp electrode contact; clean scalp required
tms: Standard TMS Safety Screening Questionnaire required
No cochlear implants within coil field

screening.name: GAD-7
screening.rationale: Gold-standard GAD screening and severity tool. 7 items scored 0–3 each. Score ≥10 = moderate anxiety (sensitivity 89%, specificity 82% for GAD diagnosis). Free, rapid, widely validated. Primary outcome measure across all GAD protocols.

screening.name: PHQ-4
screening.full_name: Patient Health Questionnaire — 4-item (Anxiety + Depression Ultra-Brief Screen)
screening.rationale: Ultra-brief 2+2 item screen for anxiety (GAD-2) and depression (PHQ-2). Score ≥3 on GAD-2 triggers full GAD-7 administration. Suitable for primary care and digital intake workflows.

diagnostic.name: HAM-A
diagnostic.rationale: 14-item clinician-rated anxiety scale covering psychic anxiety (worry, tension, concentration) and somatic anxiety (muscle tension, GI, cardiovascular). Gold-standard in clinical trials. Score ≥18 = moderate anxiety. Required for research-grade outcome documentation.

diagnostic.name: BAI
diagnostic.rationale: 21-item self-report anxiety inventory emphasising somatic symptoms. Particularly useful for capturing somatic anxiety phenotype and distinguishing from pure cognitive worry. Moderate ≥16; Severe ≥26.

baseline.name: GAD-7 (Baseline)
baseline.rationale: Primary outcome measure baseline. Establishes severity tier: Mild 5–9; Moderate 10–14; Severe 15–21. Response defined as ≥50% reduction from baseline.

baseline.name: HAM-A (Baseline)
baseline.rationale: Clinician-rated anxiety baseline for governance documentation and research-grade outcome reporting. Subscale scores (psychic vs. somatic) guide phenotype classification.

baseline.name: PHQ-9 (Baseline)
baseline.rationale: Comorbid depression is present in up to 60% of GAD patients. PHQ-9 baseline required to assess depression severity and guide concurrent treatment planning.

baseline.name: ISI
baseline.rationale: Insomnia is highly prevalent in GAD. ISI baseline documents sleep disturbance severity and tracks secondary sleep benefit from CES and HRV biofeedback protocols.

monitoring.name: GAD-7 (Monitoring)
monitoring.rationale: Primary monitoring tool. Administer every 2 weeks during treatment course. Non-response flag: <25% reduction at 3 weeks.

monitoring.name: HRV Metrics (Session Monitoring)
monitoring.full_name: Heart Rate Variability — SDNN / RMSSD / LF/HF ratio — Session Data
monitoring.rationale: For HRV biofeedback protocol: session-level HRV metrics (SDNN, RMSSD, LF/HF ratio) document baseline autonomic dysregulation and track normalisation across sessions. Target: increasing SDNN and RMSSD over course.
monitoring.access: Requires HRV biofeedback device (e.g. HeartMath emWave Pro, Polar H10 + software)

outcome.name: GAD-7 (End of Course)
outcome.full_name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder — 7-item — End of Course
outcome.rationale: Primary endpoint. Response = ≥50% reduction from baseline. Remission = score ≤4. Minimum clinically important difference (MCID) = 4 points.

outcome.name: HAM-A (End of Course)
outcome.rationale: Secondary clinician-rated outcome. Remission ≤7. Captures both psychic and somatic anxiety response to treatment.

outcome.name: ISI (End of Course)
outcome.rationale: Tracks secondary sleep benefit — CES and HRV biofeedback frequently improve sleep as a secondary outcome in GAD. ISI ≤7 = remission.

neurophysiological.name: qEEG (GAD Protocol)
neurophysiological.full_name: Quantitative EEG — GAD Anxiety Biomarker Assessment
neurophysiological.rationale: Optional qEEG maps frontal beta hyperactivation, posterior alpha deficits, and frontal theta elevation characteristic of GAD. Guides neurofeedback protocol customisation. Tracks alpha normalisation across treatment.
neurophysiological.access: Requires qEEG system and certified interpreter

functional.name: WSAS
functional.rationale: 5-item functional impairment scale assessing impact on work, home management, social leisure, private leisure, and relationships. Score ≥20 indicates severe impairment. Sensitive to treatment-related functional improvement in anxiety disorders.

protocol_bundle.name: CES — Alpha-Stim M for Generalised Anxiety (FDA-Cleared)
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: A small pilot RCT in DSM-IV GAD reported anxiety-score reductions with CES versus sham using an Alpha-Stim device (Bystritsky et al., 2008, J Clin Psychiatry). A 2018 VA Evidence Synthesis Program systematic review (Shekelle et al., publicly archived on NCBI Bookshelf) concluded RCT evidence for CES was generally at high risk of bias; overall, evidence was insufficient for clinically important effects on several outcomes, with low-strength evidence for a possible modest benefit in patients with anxiety co-occurring with depression, and CES generally appeared free of serious adverse events in reported trials. Device regulatory status (U.S. FDA Class II CES devices) does not substitute for high-certainty efficacy evidence. Mechanistic claims should be treated as tentative.
references: Bystritsky A, Kerwin L, Feusner JD, et al. A pilot study of cranial electrotherapy stimulation for generalized anxiety disorder. J Clin Psychiatry. 2008;69(3):412-417.

Shekelle PG, Cook I, Miake-Lye IM, et al. The Effectiveness and Risks of Cranial Electrical Stimulation for the Treatment of Pain, Depression, Anxiety, PTSD, and Insomnia: A Systematic Review. Washington (DC): Department of Veterans Affairs (US); 2018 Feb. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK493132/

brain_target.region: Brainstem Reticular Formation / Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis (diffuse CNS modulation via transcranial/transspinal current)

parameters.intensity: 100 µA (range 50–500 µA; start at 100 µA; titrate to patient comfort — sub-perceptual threshold preferred)
parameters.electrode_placement: Bilateral earlobe clips (auricular electrodes supplied with Alpha-Stim device)

additional_params.waveform: Biphasic modified square wave (Alpha-Stim proprietary waveform)
additional_params.home_use: Permitted after initial clinic training session
additional_params.titration: Start at 50–100 µA; increase by 50 µA increments if no effect; maximum 500 µA

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: As-needed maintenance 3x/week or daily during high-stress periods; device available for home self-administration after initial supervised sessions

steps.title: Initial clinic training session
steps.detail: Clinician demonstrates device application. Patient attaches earlobe clips bilaterally. Set current to 100 µA. Patient rests comfortably during 60-minute session. Assess sensation (tingling = too high; no sensation = optimal for many). Complete first session in clinic. Discharge with device and written instructions.

steps.title: Daily home session
steps.detail: Patient self-administers daily 60-minute session at home. May read, watch TV, work at desk (but not drive). Attach earlobe clips, set current to prescribed level. Complete session log (anxiety level before and after, any side effects). GAD-7 completed weekly.

monitoring_required: GAD-7 at baseline, week 2, week 4 (end of course), and 4-week follow-up
HAM-A at baseline and end of course
ISI at baseline and end of course
PHQ-9 at baseline and end of course
Daily session log (pre-session and post-session anxiety rating 0–10)
Adverse event log (dizziness, nausea, skin irritation at electrode sites)
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Common: mild tingling at electrode sites (adjust current down if uncomfortable), slight dizziness during first few sessions (usually resolves with current reduction). Rare: skin irritation at earlobe contact sites. Very rare: headache, nausea. All serious AEs must be reported to prescribing clinician.
escalation_rules: GAD-7 reduction <25% at week 2: review electrode placement, current level, and daily compliance; consider increasing to 200 µA if tolerated
Skin irritation at electrode sites persisting >3 days: reassess electrode placement and pressure; consider gel-based electrode covers
PHQ-9 increase ≥5 points at any monitoring point: urgent clinician review — consider parallel antidepressant treatment

No improvement (GAD-7 <4 point reduction) at end of 4-week course: consider adding HRV biofeedback or neurofeedback; reassess diagnosis, medications, and guideline-based psychotherapy; refer for specialist psychiatric care if indicated

governance.notes: FDA device clearance confirms marketing status, not magnitude of clinical benefit. Clinician sign-off required given modest/uncertain trial evidence per VA 2018 systematic review. Home use only after training and documented screening.

protocol_bundle.notes: Distinguish CES devices from conventional TENS: waveform, intensity, and intended use differ. Adherence should be tracked; benefits, when present, should be evaluated with standardized anxiety measures. Patients with pure GAD without depression were underrepresented in some CES trial syntheses.

protocol_bundle.name: HRV Biofeedback — Resonance Frequency Breathing for GAD
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: Meta-analyses of heart rate variability biofeedback report medium-to-large within- and between-group effect sizes for self-reported stress and anxiety across heterogeneous RCTs (Goessl et al., 2017, Psychol Med). A broader systematic review/meta-analysis of HRV biofeedback RCTs (Lehrer et al., 2020, Appl Psychophysiol Biofeedback) reported benefits across outcomes, with caveats that inactive-control comparisons can inflate apparent effects. Evidence specific to DSM-defined GAD as the sole diagnosis is thinner than the general anxiety-symptoms literature. Mechanisms commonly invoked (baroreflex/resonance breathing) are biologically plausible but not fully proven in clinical trials.
references: Goessl VC, Curtiss J, Hofmann SG. The effect of heart rate variability biofeedback training on stress and anxiety: a meta-analysis. Psychol Med. 2017;47(15):2578-2586.
Lehrer PM, Gevirtz R. Heart rate variability biofeedback: how and why does it work? Front Psychol. 2014;5:776.

Lehrer PM, Kaur K, Sharma A, Shah K, Huseby R, Bhavsar J, Sgobba P, Zhang Y. Heart Rate Variability Biofeedback Improves Emotional and Physical Health and Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta Analysis. Appl Psychophysiol Biofeedback. 2020;45(3):109-129.

brain_target.region: Prefrontal Cortex — Amygdala Circuit (via vagal afferent stimulation)
brain_target.eeg_position: N/A (cardiac/respiratory biofeedback — no EEG electrode required)

parameters.frequency_hz: 0.1 Hz (resonance frequency breathing — approximately 6 breaths per minute for most adults)
parameters.electrode_placement: Photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor on finger or earlobe, or ECG chest lead — records RR intervals for HRV calculation

additional_params.target_breathing_rate: 4.5–7 breaths per minute (individually determined resonance frequency)
additional_params.resonance_frequency_determination: Initial session: test 4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5 breaths/min; identify rate producing maximum HRV amplitude
additional_params.feedback_display: Real-time HRV waveform or pacing bar on screen/tablet
additional_params.home_practice: 5 minutes twice daily at resonance frequency between clinic sessions

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Daily home practice of 5–10 minutes resonance breathing continues indefinitely as a self-regulation skill

steps.title: Baseline HRV measurement
steps.detail: Attach PPG or ECG sensor. Record 5-minute baseline HRV at rest (normal breathing). Calculate SDNN, RMSSD, LF/HF ratio. Document as session baseline.

steps.title: Resonance frequency determination (session 1 only)
steps.detail: Guide patient through 6 breathing rates (4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0 breaths/min), 3 minutes each. Identify rate producing maximum SDNN amplitude — this is the individual resonance frequency. Record in patient file.

steps.title: Resonance frequency breathing with biofeedback
steps.detail: Guide patient to breathe at their resonance frequency using on-screen pacer. Provide real-time HRV waveform feedback. Coach patient to maximise HRV amplitude. Typical target: SDNN >50 ms during session.

steps.title: Post-session debrief and home practice instruction
steps.detail: Review session HRV data with patient. Assign home practice (5 minutes twice daily at resonance frequency). Log session metrics. Assess anxiety level (0–10) before and after session.

monitoring_required: GAD-7 at baseline, week 2, week 4, week 8 (end of course)
HAM-A at baseline and end of course
HRV session metrics logged every session (SDNN, RMSSD, LF/HF ratio)
ISI at baseline and end of course
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: HRV biofeedback is very low risk. Monitor for: dizziness or light-headedness from breathing changes (slow the pace, ensure patient is not hyperventilating), increased anxiety if breathing exercises trigger panic (reassure, slow down; consider concurrent CBT). No serious AEs expected.
escalation_rules: GAD-7 no improvement at week 4: check home practice compliance and breathing technique; consider increasing session frequency to 4x/week
Patient experiencing panic symptoms during breathing exercises: slow breathing rate, teach diaphragmatic breathing technique first, refer for CBT concurrent support
PHQ-9 increase ≥5 points: clinician review — consider comorbid depression requiring pharmacological treatment

governance.notes: EV-B off-label protocol. Off-label acknowledgement required. High tolerability and safety profile. Patient export permitted. Home practice component is a core element of efficacy.

protocol_bundle.notes: HRV biofeedback is ideal for somatic anxiety and hyperarousal phenotypes. The skill of resonance breathing is learned progressively across sessions and maintained by daily home practice. Patients should be advised that benefit accrues over weeks and is sustained through ongoing practice.

protocol_bundle.name: Neurofeedback — Alpha Up-Training for GAD
protocol_bundle.evidence_summary: Posterior alpha up-training (increasing 8–12 Hz power at Pz/Oz) has been investigated as a neurofeedback approach for anxiety disorders. Alpha waves are associated with a calm, alert resting state; reduced posterior alpha is a consistent finding in high-anxiety states. Kerson et al. (2009) and Hammond (2005) report promising results. Evidence is primarily from open-label studies and small RCTs. EV-C: Emerging evidence — requires clinician sign-off. Biologically plausible based on alpha suppression as an anxiety biomarker.
references: Hammond DC (2005). Neurofeedback Treatment of Depression and Anxiety. J Adult Dev, 12(2–3), 131–137.
Kerson C et al. (2009). Alpha Suppression and Symmetry Training for Generalized Anxiety Symptoms. J Neurother, 13(3), 146–155.

parameters.inhibit_band: High Beta 20–30 Hz (inhibit cognitive rumination and anxiety-related high beta)
parameters.feedback_modality: Audio and visual feedback (colour display or ambient soundscape)
parameters.electrode_sites: Active: Pz or Oz; Reference: A1 or A2; Ground: Fpz

session_structure.maintenance_sessions: Booster sessions 1x/week for 4 weeks post-course if GAD-7 regression >50% of treatment gain

steps.title: Scalp preparation and electrode placement
steps.detail: Apply conductive paste at Pz/Oz. Impedance check <5 kΩ. Reference to A1/A2. Record 3-minute eyes-open baseline EEG to confirm alpha deficit at Pz.

steps.title: Alpha up-training
steps.detail: 4–5 runs of 5-minute alpha up-training. Patient rewarded for producing alpha >8 Hz at threshold. Encourage relaxed mental state (not forced relaxation). Monitor for drowsiness — if patient falls asleep, alpha will increase but learning will not occur.

steps.title: Post-session review
steps.detail: Review reward rate and alpha amplitude achieved. Log GAD anxiety rating (0–10) before and after session. Document any adverse effects. Update threshold for next session.

monitoring_required: HAM-A at baseline and end of course
Session alpha power metrics logged every session
protocol_bundle.adverse_event_monitoring: Very low risk. Monitor for: drowsiness during session (common with alpha training — ensure patient is not sleep-deprived before sessions), mild headache, transient mood changes.
escalation_rules: No GAD-7 improvement after 10 sessions: reassess electrode placement, consider switching to alpha/theta protocol or adding CES adjunct
Significant mood deterioration (PHQ-9 increase ≥5): clinician review

governance.notes: EV-C: Clinician sign-off required. Patient export blocked pending clinician approval. Off-label acknowledgement required.

protocol_bundle.notes: Alpha up-training is appropriate for the cognitive worry-dominant phenotype where frontal alpha and posterior alpha deficits are documented on qEEG. Most effective when qEEG-guided. Consider as adjunct to CES or HRV biofeedback rather than standalone treatment.

device_options.name: Alpha-Stim M
device_options.cleared_indication: U.S. FDA Class II cranial electrotherapy stimulator devices have been cleared via the 510(k) pathway (substantial equivalence to predicate devices). Exact indications, contraindications, and device identifiers are stated on manufacturer labeling and FDA device listings — verify for the specific model and software version in use.
constraints: Use only with Alpha-Stim proprietary earlobe electrodes and electrode solution
Do not use with cardiac pacemakers or implanted defibrillators
Start at minimum current (50 µA) for first session; titrate upward
Device should not be used while driving or operating machinery
device_options.notes: Prescription status varies by jurisdiction. Confirm local regulatory labeling, indications, and contraindications before patient-facing claims.

device_options.name: HeartMath emWave Pro / Inner Balance
device_options.cleared_indication: FDA Class II biofeedback device (510(k) K004547). Indicated for stress/relaxation biofeedback. Not specifically cleared for anxiety disorder treatment.
constraints: emWave Pro for clinic use; Inner Balance for home/mobile use
HRV metrics rely on regular cardiac rhythm — arrhythmia invalidates LF/HF measures
Ensure patient understands app and home device before discharge
device_options.notes: HeartMath devices are the most widely used HRV biofeedback platforms in clinical settings. Polar H10 chest strap with compatible software is an alternative for higher-precision HRV recording.

device_options.name: Neurofeedback System (Emotiv / NeuroAmp / Neurofeedback Alliance platform)
device_options.cleared_indication: Biofeedback devices are generally FDA Class II (510(k)). Specific neurofeedback platform FDA status varies by manufacturer.
constraints: Must support alpha band (8–12 Hz) and high-beta band (20–30 Hz) reward/inhibit configuration
Impedance monitoring required — all channels <5 kΩ
qEEG-capable system preferred for personalised alpha threshold setting
device_options.notes: Any clinical-grade neurofeedback system with real-time alpha/beta feedback capability is suitable. Clinical certification of operator (e.g. BCIA certification) recommended.

brain_targets.rationale: CES devices deliver low-intensity currents via cranial/auricular electrodes; proposed mechanisms (brainstem/HPA/autonomic modulation) remain incompletely validated in humans and should not be overstated.
brain_targets.evidence_note: Mechanistic understanding of CES in humans is limited; imaging studies exist but do not establish clinical efficacy.

brain_targets.rationale: Posterior alpha (8–12 Hz) at Pz/Oz reflects tonic relaxation and attentional disengagement. Reduced posterior alpha is a consistent qEEG finding in anxiety states. Alpha up-training aims to restore normal posterior alpha levels, producing an anxiolytic effect correlated with subjective relaxation.
brain_targets.network: Default Mode Network / Resting State Network
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Increase posterior alpha (8–12 Hz) amplitude at Pz/Oz; reduce high-beta (>20 Hz) at frontal sites
brain_targets.evidence_note: Alpha suppression is a validated anxiety biomarker. Alpha up-training at Pz is the most established neurofeedback approach for anxiety disorders.

brain_targets.rationale: GAD involves reduced vmPFC inhibitory control over the hyperactive amygdala. HRV biofeedback strengthens this top-down regulatory circuit via baroreflex stimulation and vagal afferent input to the vmPFC. The resonance frequency breathing optimises baroreflex gain and parasympathetic tone.
brain_targets.qeeg_target: Reduce frontal beta hyperactivation; increase frontal alpha symmetry
brain_targets.evidence_note: vmPFC-amygdala dysregulation is the core neurobiological model of GAD (Bishop, 2007; Etkin et al., 2010). HRV biofeedback effect on this circuit is supported by fMRI studies.

sections.title: Condition Overview and Neurobiological Rationale
content: GAD involves amygdala hyperactivation with reduced vmPFC inhibitory control, ANS dysregulation (low HRV, sympathetic dominance), and qEEG biomarkers of elevated frontal beta and reduced posterior alpha.

Evidence hierarchy (this package): HRV biofeedback meta-analytic support for anxiety symptoms is relatively stronger than CES trial evidence, but both are adjunctive and heterogeneous (see protocol evidence summaries). Alpha-Stim CES is FDA device-cleared in the U.S.; regulatory clearance is not the same as large-effect, low-bias RCT evidence (VA 2018 systematic review). Neurofeedback remains emerging/off-label.
No transcranial magnetic stimulation protocol is included here; evidence and labeling for GAD are not treated as established in this package.

Phenotype-informed protocol selection remains pragmatic: high arousal may suit CES or HRV; somatic emphasis may suit HRV; cognitive worry emphasis may suit neurofeedback adjuncts — all require clinician judgment.

sections.title: Protocol Selection Guide
content: Step 1: Confirm GAD-7 ≥10 (moderate+) and DSM-5 GAD diagnosis.
Step 2: Safety screen — pacemaker (contraindication for CES), cardiac arrhythmia (relative for HRV biofeedback), seizure history.
Step 3: Identify predominant phenotype using HAM-A subscales (psychic vs. somatic) and clinical interview.
Step 4: Discuss adjunct options — PRO-CES-GAD-01 (CES) and/or PRO-HRV-GAD-01 (HRV biofeedback) based on phenotype, safety screen, patient preference, and local access.
Step 5: Add PRO-HRV-GAD-01 for somatic/hyperarousal phenotype or poor CES response.
Step 6: PRO-NF-GAD-01 as adjunct when qEEG confirms posterior alpha deficit and cognitive worry is primary.

sections.title: Monitoring and Response Criteria
content: Primary outcome: GAD-7. Response = ≥50% reduction. Remission = GAD-7 ≤4.
Administer GAD-7 every 2 weeks during treatment.
Monitor ISI as secondary measure — CES and HRV biofeedback frequently improve sleep as secondary benefit.
Monitor PHQ-9 — comorbid MDD in up to 60%; antidepressant treatment may need to run concurrently.
HAM-A subscales guide phenotype monitoring: psychic anxiety subscale tracks worry/tension; somatic subscale tracks physiological symptoms.

sections.title: Adverse Events and Safety
content: CES: very low AE rate. Common: mild scalp/earlobe tingling (adjust current). Rare: dizziness, headache.
HRV biofeedback: minimal risk. Watch for hyperventilation during breathing exercises — ensure nasal breathing and appropriate rhythm.
Neurofeedback: very safe. Watch for increased fatigue or drowsiness post-session (alpha induction can cause relaxation response).
Document all AEs in session log. Escalate if PHQ-9 worsens significantly.

patient_guide.title: Your Anxiety Treatment Guide — Alpha-Stim CES and HRV Biofeedback

sections.title: Understanding Your Treatment
content: Your treatment uses two complementary approaches to calm your nervous system: Alpha-Stim CES (a gentle electrical device you use at home) and/or HRV biofeedback (a breathing training technique).
Both are generally non-invasive and have been evaluated in research, but benefits vary by person and study quality differs.
CES devices such as Alpha-Stim have U.S. FDA device clearance for certain indications; clearance means the device may be marketed for those indications — it does not guarantee that you will respond.

sections.title: How to Use Alpha-Stim at Home
content: Clip the small electrodes to both earlobes. Apply the included conducting solution to the clips first.
Turn the device on. Set the current to your prescribed level (usually starting at 100 µA).
Use for 60 minutes daily. You can read, watch TV, or work quietly during the session.
Most people feel nothing, or a very slight tingling. If it feels uncomfortable, turn the current down.
Record how anxious you feel before and after each session in your diary.

sections.title: HRV Breathing Practice
content: Your clinician will identify your personal resonance breathing rate — this is the number of breaths per minute that most effectively calms your nervous system.
Practice this breathing rate for 5 minutes twice a day at home between clinic sessions.
During clinic sessions, a screen will show you your heart rhythm in real time so you can see your breathing's effect.
With practice, this slow breathing becomes a skill you can use any time you feel anxious.

sections.title: What to Expect Over Your Treatment Course
content: Many people notice reduced anxiety within the first week of daily CES use.
HRV biofeedback benefits build progressively over 8 weeks — regular home practice is essential.
Your clinician will check your GAD-7 score every 2 weeks to track your progress.
Continue your medication as prescribed — these treatments work alongside medication, not instead of it.
Contact your clinician if you feel significantly more anxious, depressed, or unwell at any point.

technician_sop.title: GAD Neuromodulation — Technician Standard Operating Procedure

sections.title: Alpha-Stim CES — Clinic Session Setup
content: 1. Confirm patient identity (two identifiers).
2. Check earlobe skin condition — no active lesions or infection.
3. Apply Alpha-Stim conducting solution to earlobe clips.
4. Attach clips bilaterally to earlobes. Confirm secure contact.
5. Set current to prescribed level (default: 100 µA). Check device battery.
6. Start 60-minute session. Confirm patient comfort. Remain available.
7. Record pre-session anxiety (0–10) and post-session anxiety in session log.
8. For home device training: demonstrate device application to patient; confirm patient can independently apply and start device before discharging device.

sections.title: HRV Biofeedback — Session Procedure
content: 1. Confirm patient identity.
2. Attach PPG sensor to patient's earlobe or index finger. Confirm signal quality (clean RR-interval trace).
3. Record 5-minute baseline HRV at normal breathing. Note SDNN and RMSSD.
4. Open HRV biofeedback software. Set breathing pacer to patient's prescribed resonance frequency.
5. Guide patient through resonance breathing for 20 minutes with real-time feedback.
6. Record session HRV metrics (SDNN, RMSSD, LF/HF ratio at resonance). Document in session record.
7. Assign home practice reminder: breathing rate, duration (5 minutes twice daily).
8. Record pre-session and post-session anxiety rating (0–10).

sections.title: Escalation and AE Reporting
content: Report to supervising clinician: dizziness lasting >10 minutes post-CES; skin irritation at earlobe sites; patient reporting worsening anxiety or mood.
Hyperventilation during breathing exercises: slow the breathing pace, switch to diaphragmatic guidance, ensure patient is breathing through nose.
Clinician referral triggers: PHQ-9 increase ≥5 points; patient reporting suicidal thoughts (emergency protocol); GAD-7 not improving at 2-week review.

assessment_schedule.timing: Baseline; week 2; week 4 (end of course for CES); end of course for longer protocols

assessment_schedule.frequency: Once at start, once at end
Once at start, once at end
Once at start, once at end

response_thresholds.deterioration_threshold: Increase of ≥4 points from any prior score

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Earlobe skin irritation or burns at CES electrode sites
adverse_event_triggers.action: Inspect site. Reduce current to minimum (50 µA). Apply electrode solution more liberally. If persists >3 days, hold CES sessions and seek medical review.

adverse_event_triggers.event_type: Dizziness or light-headedness during or after CES session
adverse_event_triggers.action: Reduce current to 50 µA. Ensure patient is seated during session. If persists, medical review to exclude orthostatic hypotension.
Stop pacer, guide patient to slow normal breathing, nasal breathing instruction. Reduce breathing pace at next session. Monitor for panic disorder.
Urgent clinician review. Consider pharmacological antidepressant treatment. Safety screen for suicidal ideation.
Immediate clinician contact. Safety plan. Emergency services if imminent risk. Do not allow patient to leave unsupported.

escalation_rules.trigger: GAD-7 <25% improvement at week 3 of CES course
escalation_rules.action: Clinician review. Check device use compliance (session logs). Consider increasing CES current (200 µA). Add HRV biofeedback if not already included.

escalation_rules.trigger: GAD-7 deterioration (increase ≥4 points from best score)
escalation_rules.action: Clinician review within 48 hours. Assess for life stressors, medication changes, or comorbidity emergence.
Emergency clinician review. Safety planning. Consider psychiatric referral.

escalation_rules.trigger: PHQ-9 ≥15 (moderate-severe depression) at any monitoring point
escalation_rules.action: Clinician review. Parallel antidepressant treatment may be required. Consider referral to psychiatrist.

adherence_rules.action_on_low_adherence: Clinician outreach. Review barriers (device comfort, side effects, logistics). For CES home users: phone check-in if 3 consecutive days missed. Offer flexibility in scheduling for clinic-based protocols.

sections.title: Diagnosis and Severity
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise GAD diagnosis, primary symptom phenotype (hyperarousal, somatic, cognitive worry, mixed), severity tier (GAD-7 score), and duration of illness.

sections.title: Treatment History
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Document prior medication trials (SSRIs, SNRIs, buspirone, benzodiazepines — name, dose, duration, response), CBT engagement, and any prior biofeedback or neuromodulation.

sections.title: Safety Screening and Protocol Selection
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Confirm safety screen. Note any contraindications. State selected protocol and rationale based on phenotype and clinical history.

sections.title: Sessions and Adherence
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Report sessions completed and adherence rate, including home CES compliance and daily practice logs.

sections.title: GAD-7 Score Trajectory
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Report current GAD-7 score and percentage change from baseline. Classify as response, partial response, or non-response.

sections.title: Tolerability and Side Effects
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise any adverse events and any treatment modifications made.

report_templates.name: GAD Treatment — End of Course Report

sections.title: Treatment Summary
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Summarise protocol delivered, sessions completed, and course duration.

sections.title: Outcome Measures
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Report all end-of-course scores and response classification. Note secondary sleep and depression benefits.

sections.title: Recommendations
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: State recommendations for ongoing maintenance CES use, home breathing practice continuation, medication review, and follow-up timeline.

sections.title: Subjective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Patient-reported anxiety level today (0–10), sleep quality last night, medication compliance, and whether home CES/breathing practice was completed.

sections.title: Objective
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Session number, CES parameters or HRV metrics delivered, and session compliance.

sections.title: Assessment
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Session tolerance, any adverse events, and GAD-7 score if administered today.

sections.title: Plan
sections.clinician_narrative_prompt: Next session plan, any parameter adjustments (CES current, HRV breathing rate), and clinical follow-up needs.

home_program_templates.name: GAD Alpha-Stim CES Home Program
home_program_templates.device_required: Alpha-Stim M (prescribed device with earlobe clips)
patient_instructions: Apply the conducting solution provided to both earlobe clips before attaching to your ears.
Attach one clip to each earlobe. Ensure the clip is in good contact with the skin.
Turn the Alpha-Stim on. Set the current level to your prescribed setting (shown on your instruction card).
Sit comfortably. You can read, watch television, or do quiet work during the 60-minute session.
Do not drive, use machinery, or do vigorous exercise during the session.
After the session, note your anxiety level (0–10) in your diary. Record any side effects.
Use the device daily, at the same time each day if possible (morning is preferred by many patients).
Bring your diary to every clinic review appointment.
safety_checklist: I do not have a cardiac pacemaker or implanted defibrillator
My earlobes are free from open wounds or active infection today
I am not planning to drive or operate machinery during or immediately after this session
I feel safe and am not experiencing a mental health crisis
reporting_requirements: Any discomfort or side effects experienced
contraindications_for_home: Cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator present — do not use CES at home; contact clinic
Active earlobe skin infection or wound — hold session, contact clinic
Feeling significantly more depressed, anxious, or having thoughts of self-harm — call clinic or emergency services
home_program_templates.evidence_note: Home use should follow manufacturer instructions and clinic training. Trial evidence for CES is modest/uncertain overall; adherence should be paired with objective outcome tracking (e.g., GAD-7).

consent_documents.name: GAD Neuromodulation — General Informed Consent
key_disclosures: GAD diagnosis confirmed and neuromodulation treatment plan discussed
CES devices used here are FDA-regulated cranial electrotherapy stimulators; verify the exact device labeling for indications and risks
Treatment involves daily 60-minute CES sessions and/or clinic-based HRV biofeedback
Common side effects of CES: mild tingling at electrode sites, rare dizziness
CES is contraindicated with cardiac pacemakers — confirmed absent
Treatment works best alongside ongoing psychological therapy and medication as prescribed
I can withdraw consent and stop treatment at any time

consent_documents.name: Alpha-Stim CES Safety Screening
key_disclosures: Cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator: absent — confirmed
Active skin condition at earlobe sites: assessed and cleared
Current medications reviewed including any that lower seizure threshold
Pregnancy status confirmed
I understand I should not use the device while driving

consent_documents.name: Home Device Agreement — Alpha-Stim CES
consent_documents.when_required: Before device is dispensed for home use
key_disclosures: I have received training on safe and correct device use from a qualified clinician
I understand the prescribed current setting and session duration
I will not lend the device to another person
I will complete the daily session diary and bring it to clinic appointments
I will contact the clinic if I experience persistent side effects or worsening symptoms
I will return the device if requested by the clinic
I understand the device is for my personal use only as prescribed

consent_documents.name: Off-Label Acknowledgement — HRV Biofeedback and Neurofeedback
consent_documents.when_required: Before first session of PRO-HRV-GAD-01 or PRO-NF-GAD-01
key_disclosures: HRV biofeedback and neurofeedback are not FDA-cleared specifically for GAD treatment
These protocols are used off-label based on published scientific literature with heterogeneous quality (see protocol evidence grades)
Benefits are not guaranteed; evidence for GAD specifically may be weaker than for anxiety symptoms in general
A clinician has discussed the evidence base, alternatives, and risks with me
Where applicable, FDA-cleared CES devices have been discussed as a separate adjunct option with their own evidence limitations
